description: Intermittent FMLA for ongoing treatment support

conversation.content: My wife is in outpatient treatment for alcohol addiction — 3 days a week. I need to drive her to appointments and sometimes she has bad days where I need to stay home with her. Can I use FMLA for that even though it's not all at once?

must_explain: FMLA can be taken intermittently — doesn't have to be continuous block
intermittent FMLA covers recurring treatment appointments
also covers flare-ups or bad days related to the condition
still 12 weeks total but counted in hours used
employer must be notified of foreseeable appointments when possible
employer cannot penalize for using intermittent FMLA
must_not_say: FMLA only works as a block of time
you can't use it for individual days

description: Short-term disability for person entering treatment

conversation.content: My husband has employer-provided short-term disability insurance. He's about to enter a 30-day residential program for cocaine addiction. Can he use his STD benefits to get paid while in treatment? His HR says they've never had anyone use it for 'that.'

must_explain: substance use disorder is a recognized medical condition
most STD policies cover it like any other medical condition
the key is the policy language — 'unable to perform job duties due to medical condition'
HR being unfamiliar doesn't mean it's not covered
request the actual policy document — not HR's interpretation
ADA and parity law support treating SUD as medical condition
physician documentation is needed to file the claim

description: ADA reasonable accommodation for returning to work after treatment

conversation.content: My wife just finished 45 days of residential treatment and is stepping down to IOP. IOP is 3 afternoons a week. She wants to go back to work but needs to leave early those 3 days. Can she ask for that? She's worried they'll fire her.

must_explain: ADA may require employer to provide reasonable accommodation
person in recovery from SUD is generally protected under ADA
modified schedule for ongoing treatment is a common reasonable accommodation
employer must engage in interactive process to discuss accommodation
she doesn't need to disclose specific diagnosis — just that she has a medical condition requiring treatment
cannot be fired for requesting reasonable accommodation
must_not_say: they can fire her for any reason
she has to tell them everything

description: COBRA coverage to maintain insurance during treatment

conversation.content: My son was fired from his job last week. He had insurance through them — United Healthcare. He's supposed to enter residential treatment next Monday. Will he lose his insurance? Someone mentioned something called COBRA?

must_explain: COBRA allows continuation of employer health coverage for up to 18 months after job loss
must elect COBRA within 60 days of qualifying event
coverage is retroactive — can elect after starting treatment if within the window
employer must provide COBRA notice
the cost is full premium plus 2% admin fee — can be expensive
existing coverage and pre-authorizations can continue under COBRA
must_not_say: he lost his insurance for good

description: ACA dependent coverage rules — adult child on parent's plan

conversation.content: My daughter is 24 and on my insurance plan. She needs treatment for prescription drug addiction. But she doesn't live with us — she's in another state. Can she still use my insurance? Also, when does she age out?

must_explain: ACA requires coverage for adult children up to age 26
doesn't matter if she lives elsewhere, is married, or financially independent
coverage extends across state lines in most cases
she ages out when she turns 26 (end of month or year depending on plan)
out-of-state treatment may have network implications — check in-network options in her state
plan ahead for coverage gap when she ages out — ACA marketplace, employer, Medicaid
must_not_say: she has to live with you
she can't use your insurance in another state

description: Medicaid application for treatment access — income-eligible adult

conversation.content: My brother is 35, single, unemployed, and needs treatment for alcohol addiction. He has no insurance and no income right now. We're in Michigan. How quickly can he get Medicaid and will it cover rehab?

must_explain: Michigan expanded Medicaid (Healthy Michigan Plan) — covers adults at 138% FPL
with no income, he almost certainly qualifies
can apply online, by phone, or in person — processed within 45 days max
many applications approved within days
Medicaid covers substance use treatment including residential in Michigan
can begin treatment while application is processing — Medicaid can backdate 3 months
hospitals and treatment centers often have Medicaid enrollment navigators
must_not_say: he'll have to wait months for coverage

description: EAP limitations and when to go beyond EAP

conversation.content: I used our EAP and they gave us 6 free sessions with a counselor. She was nice but basically just listened and said supportive things. My son is still using. The EAP counselor said she's done what she can. That's it? Six sessions and good luck?

must_explain: EAP is designed for short-term support — typically 3-8 sessions
EAP counselors may not specialize in substance use disorders
EAP should have provided referrals for longer-term, specialized treatment
if no referrals given, call EAP back and request addiction-specific referrals
health insurance covers ongoing SUD treatment beyond EAP
SAMHSA helpline can also provide referrals

description: Financial assistance options for treatment when insurance is insufficient

conversation.content: Insurance will cover residential but our out-of-pocket maximum is $8,000 and our deductible is $5,000. We don't have that kind of money. We're a middle-class family — too much for Medicaid, not enough for a $5K surprise bill. Are there any other options?

must_explain: many facilities offer payment plans for deductible/copay amounts
ask about financial hardship programs at the facility
some nonprofit facilities offer scholarships or sliding-scale fees
state-funded treatment may be available regardless of insurance status
appeal the cost-sharing if it creates a barrier to care
SAMHSA grant-funded programs supplement insurance gaps
community foundations and faith-based organizations sometimes provide grants
must_not_say: you just have to pay it
there's no help for people with insurance
